text: Hello there, my name is Amir Hussain. I'm 26 years old and it's been seven years that I'm working in software industry and Lately I have started to work in a company called Praza, which is really a good company But I feel like I'm not doing my job pretty well Also, I have started learning a new programming language called Guzo. One of my friends told me to learn Rust which is a better choice, but at the end I went with Go. So let's see what happens. I will share a couple of more things later.

title: My Presentation
cleaned_text: Hello there, my name is Amir Hussain. I'm 26 years old and it's been seven years that I'm working in software industry and Lately I have started to work in a company called Praza, which is a good company But I feel like I'm not doing my job pretty well Also, I have started learning a new programming language called Guzo. One of my friends told me to learn Rust which is a better choice, but at the end I went with Go. So let's see what happens. I will share a couple of more things later.
recommendations: Stabilize your pace for clarity: 129 WPM is generally good, but your score suggests it may vary. Practice a “pace ladder” with your presentation: rehearse one section at ~115 WPM (slower), then ~125 WPM (target), then ~135 WPM (slightly faster). Record yourself and choose the version where you sound most clear and confident, then aim to keep that pace throughout.

Turn pauses into confident, intentional breaks: with 9.3% pause time, you can make pauses work for you instead of feeling like you’re searching for words. Add planned 1-second pauses after key points and before transitions (e.g., after your main idea, before the next slide). Mark your script with “//” where you will pause, and rehearse keeping your posture steady and eyes up during the pause.

Reduce hesitation sounds with a replacement habit: your filler words are already low (great), so focus on the 1 hesitation sound by swapping it with a silent breath. Drill: speak 5 sentences, and every time you feel a hesitation coming, stop, inhale quietly through the nose, then continue. This trains your brain to choose a calm reset instead of an “uh/um.”

Boost confidence with a simple opening routine: your overall confidence (66.47) can rise quickly with a stronger start. Before presenting, do 2 rounds of: (1) 10 seconds of slow breathing (4 in / 6 out), (2) say your first two lines out loud with a slightly slower pace and clear final words. A confident first 15 seconds often lifts confidence for the whole talk.
